section: CENTRAL FILES NUMBER
text: 1960 Ten-Year-Plan Evaluation

section: Definition of Concept
text: Typically, the salt will occupy 8 to 25 vol % of the reactor core, with the graphite occupying the remainder.

section: Definition of Concept
text: The metallic container material is a nickel-molybdenum-base alloy which is compatible with both the salt and the graphite to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Definition of Concept
text: A large variety of reactor types can be constructed by using these basic materials of construction and utilizing U-233, U-235, or Pu fuel and Th or U-238 as fertile material.

section: Background
text: In 1954, the Aircraft Reactor Experiment was operated as a 2.5-Mw circulating-fuel molten-salt reactor.

section: Background
text: More than 90,000 kwhr were logged during the planned experimental program, with outlet temperatures as high as $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Reactor Types
text: The physical size of such a reactor will vary from about 4-1/2 ft in diameter and height for the lowest power levels up to perhaps 12 ft in diameter and height for a 300-MwE size.

section: Reactor Types
text: molten-salt fuel, occupying from 8 to $25\%$ of the reactor volume, flows longitudinally in the slots that surround the bars and is pumped from the top of the reactor through a heat exchanger and back to the reactor.

section: Reactor Types
text: The basic fuel-solvent salt is a mixture of Li $^{7}$ F and BeF $_2$ having a melting point of about $850^{\circ}$ F.

section: Reactor Types
text: A typical fuel for the single-region reactor would contain 4 to 15 mole % ThF $_4$ , depending on reactor diameter, and less than 1.0 mole % UF $_4$ ; the maximum ThF $_4$ addition would increase the melting temperature to about $950^{\circ}$ F.

section: Reactor Types
text: For the conversion ratio to be as high as 1.0, the reactor would have to be about 20 ft in diameter and 20 ft high.

section: Reactor Types
text: Such a large reactor would be capable of producing more than 1000 MwE in a single unit, but it is doubtful that such a large unit would be built to achieve a conversion ratio of unity when a much smaller two-region reactor will accomplish the same purpose.

section: Reactor Types
text: A blanket salt containing about 15 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF}_{4}$ surrounds the core region on all sides to a thickness of about 30 in.

section: Reactor Types
text: A two-region reactor with the core about 7 ft in diameter and 7 ft high should be capable of generating 300 MwE.

section: Reactor Types
text: When this core is surrounded by a 30-in. molten-salt blanket on all sides, nearly all the neutrons are usefully absorbed and conversion ratios in excess of 1.0 are possible.

section: Reactor Types
text: Typically, the fuel salt leaves the reactor at about $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , passes through a heat exchanger, and returns to the reactor at $1125^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Reactor Types
text: Again, typically, the intermediate coolant would enter the heat exchanger at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leave at $1175^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Reactor Types
text: Under near-optimum economic conditions, the steam throttle condition would be $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 1800 psia, yielding a net thermal efficiency for the plant of about $42\%$ .

section: Fuel Cycle
text: This will consist of leaving the fuel in the reactor for a long period of time, with removal of fission-product gases as the only on-site process. $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ would be added as necessary to replace burnup of fuel and to compensate for fission-product poisons.

section: Fuel Cycle
text: In the reactor considered by the Fluid Fuels Task Force $^2$ , this period was about nine years for a 318-MwE (net) reactor.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: Low Fuel-Cycle Cost. -- Molten-salt reactors should have lower fuel-cycle costs than any other reactor for four reasons: (1) As in all liquid-fuel reactors the burnup of the fuel is not limited either by radiation damage or by reactivity loss, so that chemical reprocessing may be more infrequent than for solid-fuel reactor systems.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: (2) A simple method exists for chemically reprocessing molten-salt fuels.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: (3) Reconstitution of fuel and blanket involves only dissolving $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{ThF}_{4}$ in the salt, with no metallurgical, ceramic, or mechanical steps.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: (4) The thermal efficiency is high.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: High Thermal Efficiency. -- The molten-salt system delivers steam at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 1800 psia, or higher if desired, and so fits modern economical steam cycles.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: In two-region reactors specific powers of 1.2 MwE per kilogram of fissionable uranium in the entire reactor and chemical-processing system are feasible.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: Within the scope of this factor, as outlined by the Ad Hoc Committee, the molten salt is open to question on the point of using expensive materials, INOR-8 and fluoride salts.

section: Justification for Pursuit of the Concept
text: The total cost of these materials at their expected price (\(3/lb for INOR-8 and about \)2000/cu ft for the base salt) ranges from about \)10/kw to $23/kw of installed capacity.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: Although, as mentioned above, no detailed design of a power plant has been made, the Fluid Fuels Task Force<sup>2</sup> attempted to estimate in so far as possible the direct material and labor costs for a molten-salt power plant.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: A reactor core 7 ft in diameter by 7 ft high is capable of producing enough heat for a 300-MwE station; the reactor core and blanket together will fit in a 13-ft-diameter reactor vessel.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: The evident reduction in sizes of components results in part from the fact that the molten salt has a volumetric heat capacity 4.3 times that of sodium, and in part from a higher source temperature.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: As a result of this comparison it is believed that the reduction in building size claimed in note 4 of Table I is justified.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: Although a more complete cost estimate is to be made by them soon, it appears probable that the fuel charges shown in section B of Table I are at least adequate for a 300-MwE plant.

section: Power Generation Costs of a 300-MwE Plant
text: The resultant projected power cost for a 300-MwE plant is about 6-1/2 mills/kwhr.

section: Breeding Potential
text: A recent comparative study<sup>5</sup> of thermal-breeder reactors has indicated that the molten-salt reactor is second to the aqueous homogeneous reactor in breeding potential.

section: Breeding Potential
text: An advanced type two-region molten-salt reactor is capable of a conversion ratio of about 1.06 with a specific power of 1.2 MwE/kg of fuel, and a doubling time of 13 years.

section: Breeding Potential
text: When operated to yield this performance, the fuel-cycle cost is estimated at 0.7 mill/kwhr, based on grouping reactors to provide 1000-MwE capacity at a single site.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (1) Chemistry of the Salts. -- Phase studies have been made for a wide variety of mixtures of fluoride salts, seeking those mixtures having low melting points, low viscosity, low neutron absorption, low vapor pressure, and the high chemical stability that prevents excessive corrosion.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The system of greatest interest involves the components $\mathsf{Li}^7\mathsf{F}$ , $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ , $\mathsf{UF}_4$ , and $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ , and this system has been thoroughly explored.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (2) Physical Properties of the Molten Salts. -- The density at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of the $\mathrm{LiF - BeF}_2$ - $\mathrm{UF}_4$ - $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ salts ranges from 1.9 g/cc for a composition with no heavy elements to about 3.5 g/cc for a salt containing about 15 mole % of $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ and $\mathrm{UF}_4$ combined.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Specific heats have been measured accurately, $^9$ and the product of the specific heat and the density does not vary much with the variations in composition to be expected in reactor fuels and blanket materials.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Thus the heat capacity per unit volume is about 1.0 cal/cc- $^^\circ$ C.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Viscosities of the salts are dependent on temperature, but generally range from 7 to 15 cp in the temperature range of 1100 to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Thermal conductivities have not been measured for the composition of greatest interest as yet, but the value presumed from other measurements $^{10}$ in salts is 2.5 Btu/hr-ft- $^^\circ$ F.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: After 5000 hr, there has been no significant trend of the heat transfer characteristics.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (3) Metallurgy of the Container Alloy. -- The alloy INOR-8 (Hastelloy N) was developed<sup>12</sup> to meet the special needs of corrosion resistance to fluoride salts, high-temperature strength, resistance to air oxidation, and ease of fabrication.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: It is a nickel-base alloy containing about $17\%$ Mo and $7\%$ Cr.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: A thorough examination of the mechanical properties has been made, including creep data for times as long as 20,000 hr.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Design-strength criteria have been established based on creep strength, and the high-temperature design strength lies between those of 304 and 316 stainless steel.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: At present, the thermal conductivity at temperatures from 900 to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is being determined more accurately.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (4) Compatibility of Salt and Container Alloy. -- Early corrosion-loop work, under the auspices of the ANP, with Inconel<sup>13</sup> showed that chromium was the alloy constituent most susceptible to attack and that it tended to be leached from the hot portions of a corrosion loop and to appear in the tube walls of the

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: INOR-8 was specifically developed to yield improved resistance to this weakening attack, and the corrosion results that have been obtained indicate that it is at least a factor of 10 better than Inconel.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Nine thermal-convection loops have been run for one year, with peak temperatures of 1250 and $1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and temperature differences in the loop of $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Five of these showed no appreciable attack on the INOR-8, with a maximum depth of surface roughening and pitting of 1/2 mil.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The maximum depth of attack of any of the loops was 1-1/2 mils.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Four pumped loops have been examined to date, and at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the average attack rate is less than 1/4 mil per year.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: One loop which had been repeatedly opened to the atmosphere showed heavier attack, however, with pitting to a maximum depth of 1-1/2 mils in 14,500 hr.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (5) Graphite in Molten Salts. -- About thirty different grades of graphite have been tested in a variety of molten fluoride-salt compositions.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: At 150 psi and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the salt penetrates a normal reactor-grade graphite (AGOT) to the extent of about $14 \, \text{vol}\%$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: So-called impervious grades are penetrated to a lesser extent, and seven grades have been found with penetrations of less than $1 \, \text{vol}\%$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: A bundle of rods of one of these grades was tested in a circulating-salt loop at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for one year.[15] There was no liquid penetration of the graphite, no attack at all on the surface of the graphite, and no carburization of the INOR-8 loop.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The graphite showed a $0.01\%$ weight loss, explained by removal of adsorbed gases, and showed an average pickup of $15 \, \text{ppm}$ of U and $93 \, \text{ppm}$ of Be as a result of vapor transfer of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ and $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The oxygen can be cleaned off from the graphite by a 20-hr treatment with a flush salt $^{17}$ and also by pretreatment with ammonium bifluoride (gas) at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (6) In-Pile Tests. -- A number of circulating-loop in-pile tests of Inconel corrosion were made in the ANP program.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: These tests were carried out at high temperatures (1500 to $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) where Inconel undergoes reasonably rapid attack (7 to 10 mils/1000 hr), and so some attack was always observed.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Two circulating-loop in-pile tests using INOR-8 at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , each of about 700-hr duration, have been completed, and in neither case was any corrosive effect found.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Two small-capsule tests of graphite samples immersed in salt and encased in INOR-8 have been run.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: These were taken to rather high burnups with about $3/4$ mole $\%$ U consumed by fission.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (7) Chemical Processing. -- The fluoride volatility process $^{19}$ is directly adaptable to use with molten-salt fuels.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: In the case of molten salts, the dissolution step using HF for dissolving fuel elements is omitted. $F_{2}$ is bubbled through the salt, oxidizing $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ to $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ , which is swept out as a gas.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ may be purified by absorption in NaF beds and finally condensed as liquid $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ can be reduced to $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ in a flame reducer $^{20}$ and then introduced into the fuel carrier salt.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: In a continuous on-site chemical plant where complete decontamination is not important, some of these steps can perhaps be eliminated, with reduction of the $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ to $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ being effected as it is introduced into the salt.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: A way of recovering the base salt and separating it from at least the rare-earth fission products is to dissolve it in $90\%$ HF.[21] On the basis of laboratory tests, this method appears suitable for recovery of the LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , but not any $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ that may be present.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (8) Engineering Components. -- Sump-type centrifugal pumps $^{23}$ ranging in capacity from 2 to 1500 gpm have been developed for circulating molten salts at temperatures up to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Eight different models have been manufactured and tested, and approximately 500,000 hr of non-nuclear operation have been accumulated in the temperature range of 1100 to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The longest single pump test is still in operation after three years at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: It is believed that no further development is needed for pumps with capacities to 1500 gpm and having oil-lubricated bearings.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Nineteen heat exchangers $^{24}$ of $1/2-$ to $1-1/2-\mathrm{Mw}$ capacity have been operated for an accumulated total of 25,000 hr with molten-salt temperatures ranging from 1100 to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The maximum operating time at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ or above for a single unit was $2574\mathrm{hr}$ , and the maximum number of thermal cycles from full load to no load was $194$ .

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: A remote-maintenance facility $^{25}$ incorporating a high-temperature $(1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ mockup of a 20-Mw (th) molten-salt-fueled reactor has been constructed.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (9) Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment. -- Design for a molten-salt reactor experiment was initiated about May 1, 1960.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The reactor will be a single-region graphite-moderated reactor, approximately a right cylinder $4 - 1 / 2$ ft in diameter and $5 - 1 / 2$ ft high.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The container will be an INOR-8 vessel; the graphite a fine-grained extruded impervious type that should absorb less than $1.0\mathrm{vol}\%$ of salt.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The fuel will occupy between 5 and $10\%$ of the reactor core volume.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The reactor, together with the fuel-circulating pump, the primary heat exchanger, the off-gas system, and the preheating system, will be contained in the existing 7503 building at ORNL.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Included will be tests of graphite and INOR-8 in the reactor core.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The program outlined here is believed to be on the frugal side, but should nevertheless be adequate for reaching the goal of being prepared to build a 300-MwE thermal-breeder plant with complete confidence that it will work, and with a good background as to what the costs for it will be.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: The thermal power should be in the 30-to 40-Mw range, with the generation of electricity not a necessity.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: (c) Molten-Salt Reactor Prototype. -- The prototype size is visualized as being in the range of 150 to 200 Mw of thermal power.

section: Status of Present Technology and Development Program
text: Direct extrapolation from this reactor prototype to a plant capable of 300 MwE should be possible with a high degree of confidence, since most of the components of the prototype would be of the full size required for the 300-MwE reactor.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: 12.65-MwE (gross) Reactor. -- For a reactor as small as 12.65 MwE, simplicity in both construction and in operation must be stressed.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: A moltensalt reactor of this electrical capacity would have a thermal power of about 35 Mw, and this amount of power can be obtained without difficulty from the minimum, practical size of reactor such as the one that is planned as a 5-Mw experiment.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: The reactor is a single-region reactor about 4.5 ft in diameter by 5.5 ft high.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: experiment, 1250 gpm, but the inlet and outlet fuel temperature would be changed to 1125 and $1300^{\mathrm{O}}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Single pumps of 1250-gpm capacity would be used in both the primary and secondary salt loops.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: With enriched-uranium additions, the fuel salt should last from 10 to 20 years before it would be necessary to replace it.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: The inventory of U-235 would be about 40 to $50\mathrm{kg}$ .

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: With a single-region reactor this small, there would be a high neutron leakage so that the conversion ratio would be quite low--in the neighborhood of 0.25.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: However, with the high thermal efficiency of this reactor, the fuel burnup cost is relatively low, about 2 mills/kwhr.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Whether a molten-salt reactor as small as 12.65 Mw has any legitimate use depends most strongly on the capital costs and on the reliability of its operation, since it is clear that the fuel-cycle cost will not be excessive.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: The MSRE will go a long way to answering these questions, but until the design of the reactor is complete, it is impossible to estimate capital costs within a factor of 2.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: 44-MwE (gross) Reactor. -- In this size range there are two possible designs for a molten-salt reactor.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: One is essentially the same as that described above for the 12.65 MwE size.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Thus the reactor diameter and height would be about 7 ft, and the fuel would be pumped at about 4500 gpm.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: The fuel-cycle cost would be decreased by about 0.25 mill/kwhr as a result of the slightly higher conversion ratio.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: The 44-MwE size is large enough to allow consideration of a two-region (breeder) reactor.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Physically the reactor would be the same as that described for the 300-MwE size, except that the core diameter would be about 5 ft instead of 7 ft, the blanket thickness would be trimmed to about 2 ft, and the fuel-managing scheme would be simpler.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: No special attempt would be made to achieve a high conversion ratio, but a minimum-size volatility plant would probably be large enough to achieve a conversion ratio of 1.0, and thus enable self-sustaining operation with only thorium feed.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Whether it would be more economical to use a single-region reactor or a two-region reactor in the 44-MwE size depends on the cost of construction and operation of the minimum chemical plant.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: It is reasonably possible that the minimum chemical plant can be constructed for $2,000,000, and that it can be operated for $300,000 per year.

section: Small-Size Molten-Salt Reactors
text: Thus the chemical plant adds about 2 mills/kwhr to the costs, while saving about the same amount in burnup costs.

text: There are increases in the other costs, however, caused by the presence of the blanket, as indicated in the following table of approximate fuel-cycle costs for the 44-MwE two-region reactor.
